EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of upper lows became cut off over southern and eastern Montana  extending south and east into the Dakotas and Wyoming from a  negatively-tilted trough centered off the Pacific Northwest  coastline. This cut-off low spun in place through Thursday the 20th. The result was for significant accumulating snow in eastern portions of North-central Montana, with additional heavy snow across much of the rest of Southwest and Central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at Mount Lockhart and Dupuyer creek both estimated 11 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter storm event occurred from roughly January 26 through January 28th as a powerful storm system affected much of the western United States. Periods of snow, heavy at times (especially across the mountains) occurred. Event total snow amounts of 5 to 10 inches across area valleys and the Plains were common with upwards of 2 feet of snow falling across portions of area mountains. The snow was followed by a seasonably strong arctic outbreak. Southwest Montana felt the brunt of the arctic airmass with several locations experiencing wind chill values of -40 F or colder (isolated areas having wind chill values of -50 F to -60 F), with isolated locations reaching actual air temperatures of -40 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer located near Wisdom reported a minimum air temperature of -46 F. Images of a reliable digital sensor about 2 miles west of Wisdom measured an air temperature of -52 F at 7:30 AM MST. A sensor about 6 miles southwest of Wisdom recorded an air temperature of -40 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather disturbance moved east across the region resulting in a strengthening wind field. Strong surface winds occurred for much of the late night hours of January 25th into the first half of January 26th. The strongest winds generally occurred along the Rocky Mountain Front Range as well as areas roughly along and east of a line from Havre to Lewistown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A MT Department of Transportation mesonet site (MTBWG), located in Browning, measured sustained winds of 40 mph per hour or great for at least an hour.

Event total snow amounts of 5 to 10 inches across area valleys and the Plains were common with upwards of 2 feet of snow falling across portions of area mountains. The snow was followed by a seasonably strong arctic outbreak. Southwest Montana felt the brunt of the arctic airmass with several locations experiencing wind chill values of -40 F or colder (isolated areas having wind chill values of -50 F to -60 F), with isolated locations reaching actual air temperatures of -40 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State police handled 45 slide-offs/jackknifed/stranded vehicle incidents. 12 multi-vehicle incidents. 2 incidents with jackknifed semi. Majority of these incidents occurred on US 191. SNOTEL automated equipment at Divide estimated 10 inches of snow over a 24-hour period. Elevation of the station was 7,810 feet. SNOTEL automated equipment at Black Bear estimated 12 inches of snow over a 24-hour period. Elevation of the station was 8,144 feet. SNOTEL automated equipment at Short Creek estimated 9 inches of snow over a 24-hour period. Elevation of the station was 6,993. SNOTEL automated equipment at Lick Creek estimated 11 inches of snow over a 24-hour period. Elevation of station was 6,701 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact weather disturbance brought a mix of rain, snow, and freezing rain to the area. Steady rain occurred across much of the area during the overnight hours of January 5th into the 6th. Great Falls experienced its greatest 24-hour rainfall amount on record for the month of January with the metro area and immediately surrounding locations averaging a half inch of rain. The rain transitioned to freezing rain for Great Falls and especially Choteau and Judith Basin Counties where the latter experienced a highly impactful ice storm. 100 miles of US highway were closed at the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: City of Fort Benton issued an emergency travel order. Most of Chouteau County had severe driving conditions and travel was ill-advised by law enforcement. Observer in Loma reported highly impactful freezing rain. Reported a half inch to inch of radial ice (averaging to about a half inch of flat ice) in the Loma area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee located about 6 miles SSW of Great Falls measured 0.60 inches of rain overnight. KGTF ASOS measured 0.49 inches of rain. A spotter 7 miles SW of Highwood measured and overnight rainfall amount of 0.47 inches. Water ponded in fields and yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather disturbance moved east across the region resulting in a strengthening wind field. Strong surface winds occurred for much of the late night hours of January 25th into the first half of January 26th. The strongest winds generally occurred along the Rocky Mountain Front Range as well as areas roughly along and east of a line from Havre to Lewistown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT Department of Transportation mesonet site (MTBRN) at Two Medicine Bridge measured a peak non-thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds started initially along the Rocky Mountain Front the evening of the 3rd associated with increasing mid level flow and mountain waves. Gusty winds then spread onto the remaining North-central Montana plains, primarily behind terrain features.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient Weather Station LJ Johnson Farms measured a 66 and 73 mph gust associated with a mountain wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two pushes of strong westerly winds developed along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains Feb 6 into the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTBWG measured multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a 71 mph gust.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level westerly flow increased once again, resulting in strong lee side troughing and resulting winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient Weather sensor at LJ Johnson Farms measured a two gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a 79 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level westerly flow increased once again, resulting in strong lee side troughing and resulting winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lewis and Clark Portable RAWS TR276 measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak gust of 70 mph.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel automated equipment at Shower Falls estimated 13 inches of snow over 15 hours. Station elevation: 8446 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic front brought significantly colder temperatures and combined with blustery winds, resulted in patchy extreme wind chills during the day February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTMDP MacDonald Pass measured a brief wind chill of -43 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After loss of diurnal heating during the day on the 23rd of February, another round of frigid wind chills took place before Chinook winds were able to warm most areas up later on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTBHP Big Hole Pass measured wind chills less than -40 F on two instances, including a minimum of -45 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After loss of diurnal heating during the day on the 23rd of February, another round of frigid wind chills took place before Chinook winds were able to warm most areas up later on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sensors measured wind chills of -40 F, including AWOS station K1AM Alpha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough breaking down a ridge brought strong zonal flow to the region. Weak ridgetop stability resulted in strong winds along the immediate eastern slopes and out to the Cut Bank area for a brief period Sunday the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lewis and Clark Portable RAWS TR276 measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a 72 mph gust.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two sets of wind chill warning were issued the morning of Feb 23 until 11AM. Cold temps and sufficient winds resulted in a frigid morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors measured wind chills of -40 F or less, including mesonet station mtm17 Virginia City which measured a minimum wind chill of -47 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After loss of diurnal heating during the day on the 23rd of February, another round of frigid wind chills took place before Chinook winds were able to warm most areas up later on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple AWOS stations measured wind chills of -40 F or less, including K1EM Echo, K1OM Oscar, K1MM Mike, and K1LM Lima. The coldest wind chill was observed at K1LM Lima and was -46 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After loss of diurnal heating during the day on the 23rd of February, another round of frigid wind chills took place before Chinook winds were able to warm most areas up later on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sensors measured wind chills of -40 F or less, including the S Fork Judith RAWS (SFJM8) and MTMON Monarch Canyon. The minimum was measured by the RAWS and was -44 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the southwestern three quarters of Daniels County were in (D3-Extreme) drought while the northern and eastern borders of the county were in (D2-Severe) drought.  On the second week of February the southwestern three quarters of Daniels County improved to (D2-Severe) drought while the northern and eastern borders of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the northern three quarters of Central and Southern Valley County were at (D3-Extreme) drought, while the southwestern quarter was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February the northern three quarters of Central and Southern Valley County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions while the southwestern quarter improved to (D1-Moderate) drought. These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of freezing fog and low clouds moved south from Saskatchewan into portions of far northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Sidney Airport ASOS site (KSDY), visibility of a quarter mile or less was recorded for about 3 1/2 hours on the morning of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense freezing fog formed over northeast Montana, with the river valleys of the Milk, Missouri and Yellowstone Rivers being most prone for development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple episodes of intermittent dense fog with visibility of a quarter mile or less occurred at the Wolf Point Airport ASOS (KOLF). The first episode lasted for 10 1/4 hours from the early morning to the mid afternoon on the 3rd. The second episode lasted 3 1/2 hours from the evening to the early nighttime hours on the 3rd. The third episode lasted 1 1/2 hours on the morning of the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense freezing fog formed over northeast Montana, with the river valleys of the Milk, Missouri and Yellowstone Rivers being most prone for development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple episodes of intermittent dense fog with visibility of a quarter mile or less occurred at the Malta Airport AWOS (KM75). The first episode lasted for 7 hours from the late night of the 1st to the morning of the 2nd. The second episode lasted 8 hours from the night of the 3rd to the early morning on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A chinook wind and warm front moved through the area causing wind gusts over 60 mph in areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured at 9:30 AM at the BLM Zortman E mesonet site (MTM38).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense freezing fog formed over northeast Montana, with the river valleys of the Milk, Missouri and Yellowstone Rivers being most prone for development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple episodes of intermittent dense fog with visibility of a quarter mile or less occurred at the Glendive Airport ASOS (KGDV). The first episode lasted for 2 1/4 hours in the early morning on the 3rd. The second episode lasted 4 hours from the early morning to the mid morning of the 3rd. The third episode lasted 5 1/2 hours from the late morning to the late afternoon on the 3rd. The fourth episode lasted 4 1/2 hours on the early morning of the 4th.  The last episode lasted 1 1/4 hours on the  mid morning of the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense freezing fog formed over northeast Montana, with the river valleys of the Milk, Missouri and Yellowstone Rivers being most prone for development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Sher-Wood airport AWOS site (KPWD), visibility of a quarter mile or less was recorded for about 20 1/2 hours from late afternoon on the 3rd to early afternoon on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in all but the eastern edge of Western Roosevelt County was in (D3-Extreme) drought, while the eastern edge was in (D2-Severe) conditions. By the second week of February, all of Western Roosevelt County had improved to (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of freezing fog and low clouds moved south from Saskatchewan into portions of far northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Sher-Wood airport AWOS site (KPWD), visibility of a quarter mile or less was recorded for about 15 hours from late evening on the 5th to mid morning on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight west/east surface pressure gradient resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 67 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. This was followed by an unstable northwest flow aloft on Friday the 27th. This unstable flow produced bands of moderate to heavy snow. The arctic intrusion turned to an upslope event resulting in significant snowfall for the Beartooth/Absaroka/Red Lodge Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions were reported across the entire area, especially over Lame Deer Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight west/east surface pressure gradient resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. This was followed by an unstable northwest flow aloft on Friday the 27th. This unstable flow produced bands of moderate to heavy snow. The arctic intrusion turned to an upslope event resulting in significant snowfall for the Beartooth/Absaroka/Red Lodge Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 6 inches and gusty winds were reported across the area. Roads were snow-packed and icy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the southern three quarters of Northern Valley County were in (D3-Extreme) drought, while the northern quarter of the county along the Canadian border was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February the southern three quarters of Northern Valley County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions while the northern quarter of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought. |These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass along with breezy conditions led to cold wind chills across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta Airnow mesonet site (A3570), temperatures in the teens below zero and breezy winds created a peak wind chill of -40 that was recorded at 5:00 AM on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greatest impact was icy conditions in the Mission Valley on the morning of the 28th. General snowfall of 1 to 3 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black ice and reduced visibility in blowing snow were problematic on I-90 and I-15, along with secondary roads. Snow amounts of 5 to 10 inches were common with gusts of 30-40 mph. Significant drifting with plows unable to keep up occurred on I-15 south of Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass along with breezy conditions led to cold wind chills across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Big Sky Field Airport AWOS (KS85), temperatures in the teens to 20s below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse on the morning of the 30th and briefly in the early morning of the 31st. The peak wind chill of -48 was recorded at 6:55 AM on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass along with breezy conditions led to cold wind chills across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the McDonald's MT-13 DOT site (MTMCD), temperatures in the teens below zero and breezy winds created a peak wind chill of -40 at 9:30 PM on the 29th, and again from 3:16 AM to 3:26 AM on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the southern two thirds of Northern Phillips County were in (D3-Extreme) drought, while the northern third of the county was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February the northeastern quarter of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, the northwestern corner and the southeastern quarter of the county improved to (D2-Severe) conditions, while the southwestern corner remained in (D3-Extreme) drought.  At the end of the month the northwestern half of the Northern Phillips County improved to (D1-Moderate drought) while the southeastern half of the county improved to (D2-Severe) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in all of Eastern Roosevelt County was in (D2-Severe) conditions. These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in all but the far northwestern corner of Richland County was in (D2-Severe) conditions, while the far northwestern corner of the county remained in (D3-Extreme) drought.  On the second week of February the northern three quarters of Richland County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions while the southern quarter of Richland County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the northern quarter of McCone County was in (D3-Extreme) drought with the far southern border of McCone County in (D1-Moderate) drought, and the area in between was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February the northern three quarters of McCone County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions, while the remaining quarter in the south improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area, with valley cold pools in place. Cold ground temperatures, with warmer air aloft, led to freezing rain development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received from Big Fork of 0.2 of ice accumulation as of 05:19 MST on January 14th. Also, there was a freezing rain report from Columbia Falls at 07:00 MST on 1-14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area, with valley cold pools in place. Cold ground temperatures, with warmer air aloft, led to freezing rain development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Department of Transportation road conditions the morning of January 14th showed black ice and scattered ice on many area roads and highways, including I-90 both east and west of Missoula. Also ice was reported on highways 12, 93, and 200. At 07:58 MST, crashes with injuries reported on I-90 east (mile marker 89) and US-93 (mile marker 70). There were reports from the public throughout the morning indicated slick conditions on both highways and side streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area, with valley cold pools in place. Cold ground temperatures, with warmer air aloft, led to freezing rain development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 06:09 MST, severe driving conditions were reported on highway 12 from Lolo Pass to Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area, with valley cold pools in place. Cold ground temperatures, with warmer air aloft, led to freezing rain development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports indicated widespread black ice formation. Multiple crashes were reported, including a non-injury slide off on MT-141 (mile marker 27) at 08:17 MST and a crash with unknown injury on I-90 west (mile marker 165). At 09:44 MST severe driving conditions were declared on MT-141 between MM19 and MM33 and S-271 MM17 to MM22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area, with valley cold pools in place. Cold ground temperatures, with warmer air aloft, led to freezing rain development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Department of Transportation road map showed a stretch of US-2 from West Glacier to Marias Pass with black ice from freezing rain that had fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the area, with valley cold pools in place. Cold ground temperatures, with warmer air aloft, led to freezing rain development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple crashes were reported on I-90 east, including a commercial vehicle crash at 06:45 MST (mile marker 135) and a multi-car crash at 05:22 MST (mile marker 137).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed during the afternoon and evening hours on January 22nd, leading to snow covered roads on US-93 south of Missoula. A fatal crash occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatal crash occurred on US-93 at mile marker 87. The call for the crash was dispatched at 19:47 MST. This crash on the S-curves, south of Missoula, occurred as snow showers were impacting the area. Roads had become snow-covered. Observations from near the area show temperatures dropping from just above freezing to just below around the time of the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively light precipitation event led to rather impressive snow totals across the area, with really high snow to liquid ratios of 15:1 to 30:1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow total of 7.4 was reported in Condon as of 9:00 MST on February 1st. Snow led to widespread impacts on area roads, especially I-90. There was a multiple vehicle crash with multiple injuries on I-90 near Drummond during the afternoon of January 31st. A news report stated six people were transported to hospitals with serious injuries. The accident resulted in closure of I-90 west of Drummond for several hours. There were also multiple reports from the public of slick road conditions on stretches of I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-tracked supercell thunderstorm, which produced damaging wind and hail, moved across portions of southeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large Cottonwood tree was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of severe driving conditions led to multiple accidents on I-90, especially during the early morning hours of the 28th. General snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 5 hours of severe driving conditions occurred on I-90 with multiple accidents. Loss of power at Montana Snowbowl. Six inches of snow was reported at Seeley Lake with 24 inches at the Stuart Mountain SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged periods of severe driving conditions occurred on US 12 due to ice and snow from Lolo to Lolo Pass. Chains were required on Lolo Pass multiple times as well. There were multiple accidents related to road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions resulted in a two car accident on Highway 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air undercut a plume of anomalously high moisture to bring impacts from snow, flash freeze, and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions and dangerously icy conditions led to multiple accidents. General snowfall of 3 to 6 inches occurred with blowing snow and reduced visibility, especially in the Bitterroot Valley the morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air settled over the region at the end of the month resulting in extreme cold along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning lows of -15F to -25F were common in Southwest Montana. More extreme cold of -36F was felt at Butte with -39F at Georgetown Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air settled over the region at the end of the month resulting in extreme cold along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning lows of -15F to -25F were common in Southwest Montana. More extreme cold of -38F occurred at Potomac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the northern tip of Garfield County was in (D3-Extreme) drought, the remainder of the northern third of Garfield County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the southern two thirds were in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the second week of February the northern tip of Garfield County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the northern half of Central and Southeast Phillips County was at (D3-Extreme) drought, while the southern half of the county was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February the northwestern corner of the county remained in (D3-Extreme) drought, while the northeastern corner and the central part of the county improved to (D2-Severe) conditions, the southern third of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought. |These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the northwestern corner of Petroleum County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the southern three quarters of Petroleum County were in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the second week of February, the far northwestern corner of Petroleum County remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the far northeastern corner improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, and the rest of the county remained at (D1-Moderate) drought.  At the end of February, the southwestern corner of Petroleum County improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions, the far northwestern corner remained in (D2-Severe) conditions, and the remainder of the county remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the western two thirds of the Little Rocky Mountains were in (D3-Extreme) drought, while the eastern third of the Little Rocky Mountains was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February, the immediate northern border of the Little Rockies against Blaine County improved to (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the western half remained in (D3-Extreme) drought, and the eastern half remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively light precipitation event led to rather impressive snow totals across the area, with really high snow to liquid ratios of 15:1 to 30:1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals included 5 at Blankenship Bridge, 10-12 in the Swan Mountains, 9 in the mountains NW of Essex, 4 at Stahl Peak, and 6 at Big Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively light precipitation event led to rather impressive snow totals across the area, with really high snow to liquid ratios of 15:1 to 30:1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals ranged from 5-6 between Whitefish and Kalispell, 6-10 from Swan Range to Ferndale, 4-5 in the Mission Valley. Big Fork had reported totals ranging from 6 to 9.4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the southern half of Dawson County was in (D1-Moderate) drought, while the northern half was in (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the second week of February the far northern portion of Dawson County remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of February for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions in the southwestern quarter of Sheridan County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the northeastern three quarters of the county were in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the second week of February only the far southwestern corner remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the county had improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm tracking southeast through Montana into Wyoming brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to areas of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across the Little Rocky Mountains, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 8.0 to 12.0 inches. The greatest amount of 12.0 inches was reported by the public, via Facebook at 2 E Zortman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm tracking southeast through Montana into Wyoming brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to areas of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Employee reported severe driving conditions on US Highway 2 between Saco and Hinsdale.  Snowdrifts estimated at 1 foot deep were reported on multiple bridges between the locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm tracking southeast through Montana into Wyoming brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to areas of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Employee reported severe driving conditions on US Highway 2 between Saco and Hinsdale. Snowdrifts estimated at 1 foot deep were reported on multiple bridges between the locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm tracking southeast through Montana into Wyoming brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to areas of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were reported on the Montana Department of Highways travel map on US Highway 191 between the Malta South DOT webcam near the Zortman turnoff to just south of the Fred Robinson bridge.  Severe driving conditions were also reported on the map for MT Highway 66 between the Hays DOT webcam and the junction with US Highway 191.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass combined with a strong wind gradient brought bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 50 below zero to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple nights of low temperatures between -5F and -15F, with wind chills between -15F and -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of arctic air entered western Montana thanks to low pressure to the west. This brought high winds and scattered power outages across northwest Montana. Heavy snow near the front caused low visibility, slick roads and eventual drifting snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of heavy snow started to come in before daybreak along US-93 between Eureka and Whitefish. Near Stryker there was an estimated 4 inches of snow on the road that fell in only 3 hours and by 0727, the wind picked up and caused near whiteout conditions. By the afternoon a traveler reported there were drifts on Highway 37, and it took them an hour to go from the Koocanusa Bridge to Eureka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of arctic air entered western Montana thanks to low pressure to the west. This brought high winds and scattered power outages across northwest Montana. Heavy snow near the front caused low visibility, slick roads and eventual drifting snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of flash freeze in Missoula as the cold arctic air moved in and froze the wet roads. Cars were sliding off the roads and the Missoula Police issued a Emergency Travel Only alert due to whiteout conditions. A report from the public south of Hamilton reported 6 inches of heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of arctic air entered western Montana thanks to low pressure to the west. This brought high winds and scattered power outages across northwest Montana. Heavy snow near the front caused low visibility, slick roads and eventual drifting snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lindbergh Lake observer reported 10.6 inches of snow, and 8.3 inches fell in Seeley Lake during this event. The arctic cold front brought plummeting temperatures, with gusty east winds to 35 mph, and caused a flash freeze and slick roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass combined with a strong wind gradient brought bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 50 below zero to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills across the Mission and Flathead Valleys were between -10F and -20F for multiple overnight periods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass combined with a strong wind gradient brought bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 50 below zero to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills across the Lower Clark Fork Region were consistently between -10F and -25F for multiple overnight periods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass combined with a strong wind gradient brought bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 50 below zero to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula and Bitterroot Valleys experienced multiple nights of wind chills between -10F and -20F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass combined with a strong wind gradient brought bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 50 below zero to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill readings of -10F to -20F for multiple nights prompted a wind chill advisory. While there was not evidence of widespread wind chill warning criteria being met, Deer Mountain did record wind chills as low as -35F. Due to the bitter cold and wind chills, Montana Snowbowl ski area closed on 2/22 and 2/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass combined with a strong wind gradient brought bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 50 below zero to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple nights with wind chills between -15F and -25F for the Potomac and Seeley Lake region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. This was followed by a moist flow aloft off the Pacific. This moist flow aloft produced a multi-day overrunning snow event across the Billings Forecast area. Many locations received heavy snow accumulations. The storm impacted travel across the area. Highway 191 was closed for a period of time and a large portion of Interstate 90 was shut down for nearly a day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of arctic air entered western Montana thanks to low pressure to the west. This brought high winds and scattered power outages across northwest Montana. Heavy snow near the front caused low visibility, slick roads and eventual drifting snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak arctic push brought heavy snow to Marias Pass between 6 and 9 am on the 20th, then ramped up again by the evening including Essex. Points north and west received a mixture of rain and snow before the arctic push westward overnight. Severe driving conditions were reported for US-2 between West Glacier and Columbia Falls and also at Marias Pass. Total snows included 17 inches at Marias Pass, 11.2 inches at Essex and 6 inches at West Glacier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of arctic air entered western Montana thanks to low pressure to the west. This brought high winds and scattered power outages across northwest Montana. Heavy snow near the front caused low visibility, slick roads and eventual drifting snow in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds at least to 54 mph at the Glacier Park International airport were reported and could have been higher closer to Columbia Falls. A utility company experienced power outages with at least 4 of them caused by trees falling into electrical equipment. The wind damage locations included between Whitefish Lake and Columbia Falls, east side of Flathead Lake, east of Creston, and the area south of Beaver Lake. Heavy snow with 2 inch per hour rates was reported from Bigfork to Yellow Bay for at least 3 hours. Hodgson Road was closed the following night due to severe drifting and severe driving conditions were hoisted by MDT  on Highway 206 and 406. Severe drifting was reported on Mud Lake Road due to strong northeast winds coming down off the Swan Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought improvement moved northward from southeast Montana enough to remove Wibaux County from drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start February, ongoing drought conditions for all but the northern edge of Wibaux County were in (D1-Moderate) drought, while the northern edge of the county was in (D2-Severe) conditions. By the second week of February, all of Wibaux County had improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions were favorable again for widespread dense freezing fog to form over Northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple episodes of intermittent dense fog with visibility of a quarter mile or less occurred at the Beach, ND Airport ASOS (K20U). The first episode lasted for 2 hours from the late night of the 17th to the early morning of the 18th. The second episode lasted 3 hours from late night on the 18th to the early morning of the 19th.  The third episode lasted 1 3/4 hours during the early morning hours on the 20th.

The public reported an unconfirmed amount of 36 inches of snow about eight miles SSE of Bozeman.  Nearly two feet of snow fell in less than 12 hours at the Bozeman Fire Department due to synoptic and orographic affects. Montana State University measured 25 inches of snow at the NWS COOP site. Areas near Four Corner and Bozeman Hot Springs received 18 to 24 inches of snow. The Belgrade area received 11 to 12 inches of wet snow. Amounts lowered farther west of Belgrade, but remained impactful. Stranded vehicles were noted across much of Bozeman and nearby areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the region helped develop snowfall at high elevations, including at pass level. Gusty winds also impacted the higher terrain locations. The late-season nature of the event led to additional messaging considerations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two periods of impact for this storm system. During the morning and early afternoon, 06:00 MDT-15:00 MDT, snowfall rates were between 0.25 per hour and 0.50 per hour. Chains were required at Lookout Pass around 08:00 MDT. Another round of snow occurred between 16:30 MDT-18:30 MDT. Snow rates climbed to near 1 inch per hour during the evening hours. The evening snow led to chains being required at Lookout Pass around 17:45 MDT. There were also two separate vehicle accidents that led to lanes of west-bound Interstate 90 being blocked.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start April, ongoing drought conditions in the eastern McCone County bordering Richland County was in (D1-Moderate) drought while the remaining remaining northern three quarters were in (D2-Severe) conditions and the southern quarter was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the third week of April the southern two thirds of McCone County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, while the northern third with the exception of the eastern border with Richland County remaining in (D2-Severe) conditions.  The northeastern corner remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the last week of April, all of McCone County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modestly unstable environment led to the development of thunderstorms across western Montana. Conditions were favorable for the formation of cold air funnels and landspouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved through the Mission Valley, near St. Ignatius and produced a tornado as confirmed by multiple public reports and social media pictures. It briefly touched down and caused some minor property damage. Metal roofing was torn off a farm outbuilding, and minor shingle damage occurred at a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across western Montana ahead of an approaching trough. High amounts of atmospheric moisture and slow storm motion contributed to a flash flood threat, especially over recent burn scars. A few storms produced quarter size hail.

Thunderstorms developed across western Montana ahead of an approaching trough. High amounts of atmospheric moisture and slow storm motion contributed to a flash flood threat, especially over recent burn scars. A few storms produced quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm containing very heavy rain moved directly over the Thorne Creek burn scar. Radar estimated 30 minute rainfall totals between 0.55 to 0.90 inches. A report was received from the forest service that a debris flow went over the Thompson River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another afternoon of thunderstorm development resulted in a severe thunderstorm near Helmville and Ovando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm near Ovando showed a bowing structure, indicative of strong outflow winds. Velocity on the lowest radar tilt showed winds near 50 knots. Mid-altitude radial convergence was also present, adding confidence to severe wind gust potential. A report was received that the sign to the Ovando Store was blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High impact and heavy snow event. Several SNOTEL sites across the Little Belts estimated 20 to 24 inches of snow falling over a 24 hour period. A local ski resort was unable to open for business due to the heavy snow. All roads and highways in the area were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist easterly surface flow, combined with a disturbance aloft, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area. Some of these thunderstorms produced very heavy rain and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A RV Park flooded with water running across nearby roads, drainages and culverts. Some culverts were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and rainfall created a steady rise for the Clark Fork River above Missoula. River levels exceeded minor flood stage, and remained there over the course of a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clark Fork River above Missoula reached minor flood stage of 7.5 feet by 1100MST on May 3rd. The river remained above minor flood stage through 1900MST on May 11th. The river crested at 9.5 feet around 0400MST on May 7th. This did cause sandbagging efforts in the frequently flooded area of Tower and Kehrwald streets in the Orchard Homes neighborhood of Missoula.

There were multiple reports of trees down northwest of Troy along US Highway 2, near mile marker 5, including a large tree that blocked the highway. Around 2300MST, a car ran into a tree blocking part of Highway 2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions resulted in a 3-vehicle non-injury accident on MT Highway 287 in Sheridan at mile marker 35.5, reported at 9:38. Multi-car pile-up reported at 16:02 at mile marker 37 on MT Highway 287 in Sheridan. Ten vehicles were involved, and a loaded school bus was stuck in the chaos. There were six ambulatory patients. Conditions were blizzard-like, with little to no visibility. The temperature with wind chill was approximately -37. Victims were escorted to a local church to speak with Montana Highway Patrol and evaluated by EMTs. Some vehicles were left in the ditches until the weather improved for them to be removed safely. Throughout the evening and the following day, there were additional slide-offs. Two minutes after the 10-car pile-up report, another incident at mile marker 1 MT Highway 84 in Norris occurred on the opposite side of the county, reported at 16:04. This incident involved two vehicles, one of which was a school vehicle carrying numerous children. No reported injuries were involved, but road and weather conditions were terrible and deteriorating quickly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad quasi-stationary upper-level trough situated over western  CONUS lingered for several days and led to persistent southwesterly flow aloft and daily showers and thunderstorms across portions of Southwest, Central, and North-central Montana, some of which were severe, producing strong gusty winds and significant hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported wind gusts estimated between 50 and 60 mph with pea sized hail. Trees had leaf damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system resulted in a long duration snow event which was the heaviest across the Little Belt Mountains and Rocky Mountain Front. Area valleys and portions of the plains experienced blustery winds and a few inches of snow. The worst travel impacts occurred in Madison County where multiple vehicle incidents and a few injuries occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated equipment at Crystal Lake estimated 10 inches of snow over a 14 hour period, at an elevation of 6,213 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After loss of diurnal heating during the day on the 23rd of February, another round of frigid wind chills took place before Chinook winds were able to warm most areas up later on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mesonets measured wind chills of less than -40 F, including mtm53 MDA Oilmont and GW0659 Grassy Butte. The coldest observed wind chill was -46 at EW9056 Joplin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After ample snow fell along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains, Strong westerly winds developed and resulted in significant blowing snow, especially along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front and High Plains. Isolated strong wind gusts also occurred across the Madison and Gallatin Mountains as well as the Judith Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred in a data sparse area, likely resulting in near ground blizzard conditions.  A peak measured wind gust of 82 mph occurred at the Deep Creek RAWS, located about 8 miles southeast of East Glacier Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After ample snow fell along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains, Strong westerly winds developed and resulted in significant blowing snow, especially along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front and High Plains. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant blowing of snow resulted in a prolonged period of greatly reduced visibility. Strongest recorded wind gusts consisted of a 58 mph gust from a portable RAWS site located about 11 miles west/northwest of Augusta and a 75 mph wind gust from mesonet site DELMT located about 14 miles west of Bynum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a relatively wet and snowy winter, areas of drought continue to persist in northeast Montana.

These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong surface low over the Dakotas brought very gusty north to northwest winds along with snow across far southeast Montana. The combination of the falling snow and strong winds, as well as snow already on the ground, resulted in blizzard conditions across Fallon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow occurred across the area. The Baker Airport reported winds gusting 40-60 mph with visibilities of 1/4 mile. US 12 was closed from Miles City to the North Dakota border and Route 7 was closed from Baker to Ekalaka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper low and trough moved out of the Great Basin area northeast across Wyoming during this time period. At the same time, a surface low moved across Wyoming into South Dakota. As a result of all the above, a deep, easterly upslope flow was in place across the southern and south central portions of Montana. During the first 24 to 36 hours of the event, conditions were unstable with steep lapse rates. This resulted in periods of very intense snowfall during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th and through the day on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 16 inches was reported across the area. Severe travel conditions were reported on Interstate 90 and Highway 212.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above average snowfall helped to end the drought in portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start March, ongoing drought conditions in the far southwestern corner  of Sheridan County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the county was in (D1-Moderate) drought. On the third week of March, the far southwestern corner of Sheridan County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought while the remainder of the county improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a relatively wet and snowy winter, areas of drought continue to persist in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start March, ongoing drought conditions at the immediate northern border of the Little Rockies against Blaine County and the eastern half of the Little Rockies were in (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the western half remained in (D3-Extreme) drought.  On the third week of March, the immediate northern border of the Little Rockies against Blaine County and the eastern half of the Little Rockies improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, while the remainder of the western half improved to (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall for March helped to lift Dawson County out of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start March, ongoing drought conditions in the far northern portion of Dawson County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the county was in (D1-Moderate) drought. On the last week of March, the southwest corner of Dawson County improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions while the remainder of Dawson County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad quasi-stationary upper-level trough situated over western  CONUS lingered for several days and led to persistent southwesterly flow aloft and daily showers and thunderstorms across portions of Southwest, Central, and North-central Montana, some of which were severe, producing strong gusty winds and significant hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud witnessed by NWS personnel and noted in a pilot report during a climb out of nearby Great Falls International Airport. Location estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a relatively wet and snowy winter, areas of drought continue to persist in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start March, ongoing drought conditions in the southwestern three quarters of Daniels County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the northern and eastern borders of the county were in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the third week of March, the northern and northeastern third of Daniels County improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions, the middle third and southwestern portions of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, with about 20 percent of the county in the southcentral area remaining in (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist, easterly surface flow was in place across the Billings area. An area of moderate to heavy rain, with an occasional embedded thunderstorm, positioned itself over western Yellowstone County during the evening hours of the 2nd. Many areas were flooded, especially the west side of Billings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads on the west side of Billings were flooded and closed. Gable Road was particularly hit hard. Birely Drain and Big Ditch overflowed their banks washing out a portion of Gable Road. The cost to repair Gable Road will be about 600K. Total flood damage costs to the city will be around $1M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Individuals near Helena were playing disc golf outside while a thunderstorm was located nearby. A lightning strike from the nearby storm struck two men, injuring both of them. One had to be resuscitated with CPR. As of early July 2023, both were recovering from their injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two male individuals (ages 22 and 21) were struck by lightning while playing disc golf.             Both were knocked unconscious. One of the individuals regained consciousness and             performed CPR on the other, who was later flown to Salt Lake City for advanced medical             care. As of early July 2023, both individuals were recovering from their injuries. Time of event is estimated.

A pair of upper lows became cut off over southern and eastern Montana  extending south and east into the Dakotas and Wyoming from a  negatively-tilted trough centered off the Pacific Northwest  coastline. This cut-off low spun in place through Thursday the 20th. The result was for significant accumulating snow in eastern portions of North-central Montana, with additional heavy snow across much of the rest of Southwest and Central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports from the public indicated snow in excess of 8 inches fell in the Dillon area over a relatively short period (Less than 12 hours, generally). A storm total report of 11 inches was received and a report of 10 inches over 5.5 hours was also received (5:30 to 10PM MDT). The COOP station at University of Montana - Western, in Dillon, had an event total amount of 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures occurred in the first two weeks of April, with highs near 80 degrees occurring at times. These warm temps, along with occasional rain, resulted in melting of a deep snowpack on top of frozen ground which led to significant runoff into area waterways. Moderate to major flooding occurred along portions of the Milk River and its tributaries. Impactful snowmelt flooding also occurred across Southcentral Montana, in particular across the Gallatin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mountain snowpack in the Milk River basin melted out very quickly and contributed significant flooding. Virtually the entire Milk River system flooded in a matter of days as a result, starting near the headwaters in Hill County. Big Sandy Creek near Havre crested at 12.07 feet on April 13, which (as of entry) was the fourth highest crest on record. Route 50 was greatly flooded as well as some businesses . Farther north, Lodge Creek below McRae Creek  approached Major Flood stage before cresting at 10.59 feet on April 18th. Much of rural northern Hill County experienced flooding as a result. The Milk River gauge near Havre remained below flood stage, although river levels did come out of their banks farther east, ultimately impacting eastern portions of Havre.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Mid level flow mixed to the surface near to the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains and in the Madison River Valley in Southwest Montana. Otherwise, most other locations stayed below high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KCTB Cut Bank Airport measured a gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of rain and thunderstorms affected much of Montana during the first half of June. Flash flooding occurred across portions of the region, especially across Fergus County as well as the Rocky Mountains. Fergus County was hit exceptionally hard as several days of occasional heavy rain exasperated area creeks and streams. Casino Creek, located south of Lewistown, was washed out in at least two locations with significant road repairs required. Cottonwood Creek to the west of Casino Creek completely washed out Cotton Creek Road in several areas. Across the Rocky Mountains, easterly flow compressed moisture along the Rocky Mountain Front Range resulting in heavy rainfall and thunderstorms. Several debris flows were reported. Portions of Glacier National Park were temporarily closed due to rock slides and debris flows. The Helena-Lewis and Clark National Forest also reported several flooding impacts. Across Meagher and Broadwater Counties, heavy rain affected the Woods Creek and Deep Creek burn scar areas, resulting in flash flooding along Highway 12. Flash flooding also occurred just south of Great Falls along Wilson Butte Road from a rapidly developing thunderstorm that produced 1.37 inches of rain in less than an hour at a NWS employee's home along Wilson Butte Road. A brief tornado also occurred across rural Hill County and was greatly photographed, but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Meagher County Sheriff's office reported that a portion of Highway 360 west of White Sulphur Springs was closed due to flooding on the roadways.

Unseasonably warm temperatures occurred in the first two weeks of April, with highs near 80 degrees occurring at times. These warm temps, along with occasional rain, resulted in melting of a deep snowpack on top of frozen ground which led to significant runoff into area waterways. Moderate to major flooding occurred along portions of the Milk River and its tributaries. Impactful snowmelt flooding also occurred across Southcentral Montana, in particular across the Gallatin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along the Milk River across Blaine County. The Rocky Boy SNOTEL station which is the main Milk River basin station peaked later than normal and had over 3.5 times the snow water equivalent that it normally would on April 6 (10.7��� of snow water equivalent versus a normal of 3.0���). When this thick snowpack melted, virtually the entire Milk River system flooded in a matter of days, as a result. The Milk River near Harlem crested at 23.80 feet on April 12, which as of this entry was the seventh highest river level recorded at this site. Roughly 4,000 aces of irrigation land was flooded. Chinook water plant was surrounded by water. Several roads and homes were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June began with river flooding along Clear Creek near Chinook, which reached Major Flood Stage for the second time this spring. In fact, Clear Creek recorded its third highest crest on record, as of writing. Fergus County, especially locations south of Lewistown, also experienced flooding due to heavy rainfall. Elsewhere, isolated severe thunderstorms occurred as a spring-like storm system moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported hail fell for roughly 15 minutes and was of various sizes. Largest was ping pong size. Impacts from the hail to hay crops were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of rain and thunderstorms affected much of Montana during the first half of June. Flash flooding occurred across portions of the region, especially across Fergus County as well as the Rocky Mountains. Fergus County was hit exceptionally hard as several days of occasional heavy rain exasperated area creeks and streams. Casino Creek, located south of Lewistown, was washed out in at least two locations with significant road repairs required. Cottonwood Creek to the west of Casino Creek completely washed out Cotton Creek Road in several areas. Across the Rocky Mountains, easterly flow compressed moisture along the Rocky Mountain Front Range resulting in heavy rainfall and thunderstorms. Several debris flows were reported. Portions of Glacier National Park were temporarily closed due to rock slides and debris flows. The Helena-Lewis and Clark National Forest also reported several flooding impacts. Across Meagher and Broadwater Counties, heavy rain affected the Woods Creek and Deep Creek burn scar areas, resulting in flash flooding along Highway 12. Flash flooding also occurred just south of Great Falls along Wilson Butte Road from a rapidly developing thunderstorm that produced 1.37 inches of rain in less than an hour at a NWS employee's home along Wilson Butte Road. A brief tornado also occurred across rural Hill County and was greatly photographed, but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris rushed across several areas of Wilson Butte Road resulted in damage and impacts. Water flowed out of the rockier boulder terrain on the east side of the road, crossed the road with water depth being 2 to 3 feet deep at times. Water and debris entered at least two homes. One culvert was completely washed out with drainage pipes unearthed. Some wheat crops were flattened. NWS Employee that lives along the road had water enter their basement. Wilson Butte Road was impassable for approximately an hour.

The result was for significant accumulating snow in eastern portions of North-central Montana, with additional heavy snow across much of the rest of Southwest and Central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many observations of heavy snow were reported, including official NWS observations. Official NWS observations measured 6.5 inches new snow in 12 hours, and 9.3 inches storm total. The peak total was observed by a co-op observer 4 miles ese of Fort Shaw, who measured 13.4 inches over 24 hours ending 7 AM MDT the 19th. The co-op observer located three miles east/southeast of Sand Coulee had an event total amount of 14 inches. Co-op observer located about four miles south of Sun River reported an event total snow amount of 15.7 inches.

The river briefly dropped below flood stage at 7:42 PM on June 2nd, before rising back above flood stage at 3:30 AM on June 3rd. The river crested at 11.71 feet at 7:19 AM on June 3rd. The river would fall below flood stage at 10:23 AM on June 3rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Musselshell River at Mosby (MSBM8) rose above its flood stage of 10 feet at 11:13 AM on June 9th.  The river crested at 10.68 feet at 1:31 PM. The river would briefly go below flood stage at 5:42 PM on June 9th before rising back above flood stage at 6:42 PM on June 9th. The river would eventually fall below flood stage at 9:24 PM on June 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures occurred in the first two weeks of April, with highs near 80 degrees occurring at times. These warm temps, along with occasional rain, resulted in melting of a deep snowpack on top of frozen ground which led to significant runoff into area waterways. Moderate to major flooding occurred along portions of the Milk River and its tributaries. Impactful snowmelt flooding also occurred across Southcentral Montana, in particular across the Gallatin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chouteau County Sheriff's Office reported flood-related closures in Big Sandy at North Lonesome Lake Road between Ihmsen Well Road and Gardiner Road and from Black Coulee Road to Rudyard Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty westerly winds combined with low humidity to create conditions supportive of fire growth during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving grass fire was reported in a neighborhood just southwest of the town of Lolo. At the time of the fire, a nearby RAWS station reported southwest winds around 10 mph gusting between 25-30 MPH. Relative humidity ranged from 10 to 15 percent. Loss estimates include one mobile home lost and one damaged, 16 outbuildings/sheds lost, and 11 vehicles/RV���s were lost or damaged. Three civilians were treated for smoke inhalation and one firefighter suffered a knee injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific weather system brought isolated showers and thunderstorms to portions of Southwest and North-central Montana followed by gusty westerly winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 62 mph measured at 7 SW Highwood. Wind was sustained at 50 mph during the time of the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered mostly dry thunderstorms developed on the afternoon on the 28th with subsequent lightning leading to a fire that damaged a local hay crop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning caused fire consumed hay stored for winter cattle feeding at the Double K Ranch. More than 400 bales each weighting 1000 pounds were lost, which is a year's worth of feed for this ranch. Fencing was also lost in the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moved over the Northern Rockies, providing lift during peak afternoon heating. Low level dry air helped produce strong thunderstorm outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of large trees down near Lolo Pass were received. Radar velocity data indicated winds over 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moved over the Northern Rockies, providing lift during peak afternoon heating. Low level dry air helped produce strong thunderstorm outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar velocity data indicated wind speeds over 50 knots. An emergency manager reported a tree falling on a house in Target Range. A wind gust of 51 mph was recorded at the Missoula International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moved over the Northern Rockies, providing lift during peak afternoon heating. Low level dry air helped produce strong thunderstorm outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported that a large tree, approximately 18 inches in diameter, fell down one mile east of Seeley Lake. Trees were also reported down on Morrell Creek Road and 13 miles south of Condon, on Highway-83. A couple hundred customers lost power near Condon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Hilary brought deep atmospheric moisture into the Northern Rockies. A closed upper-level low pressure system provided sufficient lift for thunderstorms, with wind shear profiles favorable for organized convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at the Glacier Park International Airport. The thunderstorm resulted in a couple hundred customers without power in West Glacier and near Coram, according to Flathead Electric COOP. Numerous reports of downed trees were also received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Hilary brought deep atmospheric moisture into the Northern Rockies. A closed upper-level low pressure system provided sufficient lift for thunderstorms, with wind shear profiles favorable for organized convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple car crashes were reported on Interstate-90 near Missoula as a thunderstorm with strong winds and heavy rain moved through. A wind gust of 52 mph was recorded at the Missoula International Airport, with the main core of the storm passing to the east of the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sidney Fire (Lightning start) was a forest fire started August 1st, 2023 21 miles southwest of Browning. The fire eventually grew to be 228 acres and was active through the remainder of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 1, members or the Rocky Mountain Ranger District (USFS) reported the Sidney fire south of US-2 along the Rocky Mountain Front, between 2 and 2.5 miles west from the Badger Cabin admin site. The fire start was lightning. Timber was the main fuel involved and the fire eventually grew to the size of 228 acres. The fire lasted through the entire month of August, spanning into the second week of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed 500 mb low over the Pacific Northwest opened up and shifted toward the Northern Rockies during this period. This wave, in combination with daytime heating, resulted in showers and thunderstorms across the region August 6th. Shear was meager, but was maximized locally to produce one tornado in Jefferson County. Additionally, a landspout was observed in southern Beaverhead County near the Idaho border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager relayed photos, and several photos were received via social media, of a well documented tornado in the Whitetail Basin. No structural damage occurred and most areas were inaccessible to the NWS storm survey team.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first prominent ridge breakdown of the season resulted in strong zonal to southwesterly flow across North-central Montana ahead of a Canadian cold front. Deep mixing resulted in a few gusts in excess of 50 mph across the high plains adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front, but only one measured gust reaching High Wind Warning Criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KCTB Cut Bank Airport measured a 62 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly flow associated with the remnants of Hurricane Hilary overspread much of Southwest Montana August 21st. Although plenty of gusts greater than 50 mph were observed, no High Wind Warning caliber gusts were observed. The lone verification point was from high sustained winds at Norris Hill on the northern end of the Madison River Valley zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Norris Hill MTNOR measured sustained winds, averaged over an hour, of 42 mph. The sensor also measured a peak gust of 55 mph.

Lingering snowpack in the higher elevations of the Little Belt Mountains began to melt in early May. This melting was accelerated by moderate rainfall. Belt Creek ultimately flooded, impacting areas within the Lewis & Clark National Forest to near the community of Belt. Sluice Boxes State Park's main entrance was flooded with the park ultimately closed for a period of time. Some homes between Armington Junction and Sluice Boxes had water flooding yards and fields. Some stranded cattle were noted south of Armington Junction. Water levels between Belt and Armington flooded yards and up to a barn, but no homes were impacted. The gauge for Belt Creek near Monarch officially crested at 7.02 feet on May 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: BTCM8, Belt Creek near Monarch, crested at 7.02 feet. Sluice Boxes State Park's main entrance was flooded and closed. Water levels flooded many yards and approached many homes, but no reports of impactful structural flooding were received. Water levels were out of their banks in many areas from Sluice Boxes northward to near Belt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring rains across the area resulted in additional high elevation snowmelt which led to elevated water levels along many creeks and streams. Belt Creek flooded for a second time this month. Water levels crested at 6.78 feet at the river gauge along Belt Creek near Monarch. Water briefly impacted Sluice Boxes State Park's entrance parking lot and flooded primarily fields and driveways from Sluice Boxes northward to near Armington. Two men visiting Sluice Boxes State Park were impacted by the fast moving water current associated with the high water levels and ultimately drown. Water levels were high all the way from near Belt to near Neihart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: BTCM8, Belt Creek near Monarch, crested at 6.79 feet on May 17th. Water levels resulted in strong currents that claimed the lives of two men visiting from Canada. The two men went into the fast moving water to retrieve their dog. The dog made it to shore, but the two men were pulled downstream and ultimately drowned. Water levels also flooded a portion of Sluice Boxes State Park's entrance parking lot as well as fields and yards from Sluice Boxes northward to Armington. A house in Neihart was flooded near the area were O'Brien Creek enters Belt Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Fall Winter Storm impacted much of the Billings Forecast Area. The coldest air of the season invaded southern and southeastern Montana and northern Wyoming. At the same time, a strong upper low moved off the Pacific coast and across Idaho. This upper low brought a prolonged period of winter precipitation. Warm air aloft with freezing temperatures at the surface resulted in several hours of freezing drizzle before changing to snow. Many roads saw light ice accumulations creating dangerously slick conditions. The precipitation then transitioned to snow creating snow packed roads over icy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Washington and Oregon drew arctic air west of the Continental Divide which caused sub-freezing temperatures, periods of snow, and slick road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain followed by snow, along with falling temperatures into the 20s and gusty easterly winds to 30 mph caused flash freezing of the roadways. A CoCoRaHS observer in the South Hills reported 0.25 inches of ice underneath the 3.1 inches of snow. Other snow amounts include 3.8 inches Lolo and 6 inches on the grass 2 miles north of Wye. A secondary storm brought very heavy snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour to the Darby area during the evening of the 25th. A person from the public reported 10 inches of total snowfall in Darby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid level low tracked eastward near the Canadian border during October 16. The combination of strong westerly flow aloft and a surface low in SK producing a strong pressure gradient resulted in high winds across much of North-central Montana as well as portions of Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS station TR276 L-C Portable No. 1 measured multiple wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, including a peak gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough, embedded waves, and jet energy supported two rounds of snow across North-central and Southwest Montana October 25-27, 2023. The first round, Oct 25 & 26, was more impactful to North-central and Central Montana, while the second round of snow, Oct 26 & 27, was more impactful to Southwest through Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 10 inches of total snow on bridges from the event. Additionally, snotel equipment at Many Glacier estimated 10 inches storm total snow. Most snow at the snotel site fell in the morning through afternoon on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough, embedded waves, and jet energy supported two rounds of snow across North-central and Southwest Montana October 25-27, 2023. The first round, Oct 25 & 26, was more impactful to North-central and Central Montana, while the second round of snow, Oct 26 & 27, was more impactful to Southwest through Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at Copper Camp observed the long duration snow event. Through 10AM MDT Wednesday the 25th, 9 inches new snow was estimated. Thereafter, an additional 6 inches of snow fell, totaling 15 inches for the entire event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough, embedded waves, and jet energy supported two rounds of snow across North-central and Southwest Montana October 25-27, 2023. The first round, Oct 25 & 26, was more impactful to North-central and Central Montana, while the second round of snow, Oct 26 & 27, was more impactful to Southwest through Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of snow reaching winter storm criteria as well as impacts from the snow were received over this period. Official NWS obs at WFO Great Falls totaled 6 inches new snow for the event. Both an NWS employee and the public reported snowfall amounts in excess of 8 inches around Great Falls. NWS employees on their way in to work saw two jack-knifed semis on Interstate 15 blocking an exit ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Washington and Oregon drew arctic air west of the Continental Divide which caused sub-freezing temperatures, periods of snow, and slick road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Washington and Oregon drew arctic air west of the Continental Divide which caused sub-freezing temperatures, periods of snow, and slick road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash freeze of roadways occurred as rain changed to snow with falling temperatures into the teens. Gusty north to east winds between 20 and 40 mph caused periods of blowing snow with reduced visibility along and west of the Divide. Snow amounts include 6 inches in Ovando, 8 inches west of Anaconda and Philipsburg through the morning of the 25th. A secondary storm brought an additional 11 inches of snow to Anaconda and the 2 day total of 19 inches became the 2nd highest on record.  Butte received a total of 7.3 inches out of both storms. A plethora of vehicle accidents and semi jackknifes occurred during this time period.
