EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog impacted a number of locations in western Montana, leading to black ice on roadways and impacts at area airports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog led to ice formation on area roadways. Icy roads were considered a factor in a fatal crash that occurred on Highway 200 between Lincoln and Ovando.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog impacted a number of locations in western Montana, leading to black ice on roadways and impacts at area airports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog led to the formation of ice on area roadways. A propane truck tipped onto its side on railroad tracks at the intersection of Mullan Road and Beckwith Street in Frenchtown. Icy roads were believed to be a contributing factor to the crash. There were also multiple flights delayed and one flight diverted at Missoula International Airport due to the dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure led to stable conditions and cold pool formation across the Northern Rockies. This led to a prolonged period of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed at the Missoula International Airport around 22:25 MST on December 15, with visibility below one quarter statute mile. Visibility briefly improved to one half mile between 11:53 MST and 15:30 MST on December 16, before returning to one quarter mile after 15:30. Dense fog lasted through 0910 MST on December 17, with visibility continuing to move between one half mile and one quarter mile through 0625 MST on December 18. In total, Missoula International Airport was below airport landing minimums for around 28 hours. This led to multiple days of flight delays, cancellations, and diversions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog impacted a number of locations in western Montana, leading to black ice on roadways and impacts at area airports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed at the Glacier Park International Airport, with visibility dropping to one quarter statute mile. The fog led to 5 flight diversions and at least one cancellation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving across the region brought gusty winds and prompted a High Wind Warning across the Little Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at 11:15 AM at the BLM Glendive mesonet site (MTM28).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture settled into the region from North Dakota and generated some dense freezing fog across the far northeast corner of Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Sher-Wood airport AWOS site (KPWD), visibility of a quarter mile or less was recorded for about 5 1/4 hours on the morning of the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures occurred in the first two weeks of April, with highs near 80 degrees occurring at times. These warm temps, along with occasional rain, resulted in melting of a deep snowpack on top of frozen ground which led to significant runoff into area waterways. Moderate to major flooding occurred along portions of the Milk River and its tributaries. Impactful snowmelt flooding also occurred across Southcentral Montana, in particular across the Gallatin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lewis and Clark County Sheriff's Office reported the high bridge over the Dearborn River was washed out. Public works closed the road at Flat Creek. Several secondary roads across the area were flooded at times. Flooding across northwest Helena also occurred, especially west of Birdseye Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced some large hail along with some very strong winds.
Several thunderstorms developed across the central and western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. These thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts due to large temperature/dew point spreads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph occurred at the Livingston Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Washington and Oregon drew arctic air west of the Continental Divide which caused sub-freezing temperatures, periods of snow, and slick road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow as temperatures fell into the 20s and winds gusted to 35 mph which caused flash freezing of the roadways. A COOP in Potomac reported 4 inches of snow. A semi rolled over east of Clearwater Junction and caused Highway 200 to closed for a period during the afternoon of the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Washington and Oregon drew arctic air west of the Continental Divide which caused sub-freezing temperatures, periods of snow, and slick road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 8 inches of snow fell between Happy's Inn and Kila during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Washington and Oregon drew arctic air west of the Continental Divide which caused sub-freezing temperatures, periods of snow, and slick road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and gusty winds occurred and caused roads to be slick with ice underneath the snow. A weather spotter in Hungry Horse and COOP in West Glacier both reported 9 inches of snowfall during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over Wyoming brought a deep, upslope flow resulting in heavy snow to the Pryor and Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Close to 2 feet of snow occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record snowfall and below normal temperatures during the last one third of October,|allowed drought conditions to improve across several locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing D2-Severe drought conditions continued across the far northeastern one quarter of Garfield County, while the far northern portions of Garfield County (bordering Fort Peck Lake) remained in D1-Moderate drought. The remaining portion of the northern third of Garfield County remained in D0-Abnormally Dry.  At the end of the month, all but the northeastern corner of Garfield County improved to no drought classification, while the lower half of the northeastern corner improved to D0-Abnormally Dry.  The tip of the northeastern corner improved to D1-Moderate drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough combined with atmospheric moisture from the Pacific, led to an early season snowstorm which generated heavy snow across much of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Richland County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 8.5 to 9.0 inches. The greatest amount of 9.0 inches was measured by a COOP Observer at 2 S Sidney.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record snowfall and below normal temperatures during the last one third of October,|allowed drought conditions to improve across several locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions continued across the far southwestern corner of Western Roosevelt County which remained in D2-Severe, while the rest of the county remained in D1-Moderate.  At the end of October, the southeastern three quarters of Western Roosevelt improved to D0-Abnormally Dry, while the western border and northwestern quarter of the county improved to D1-Moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record snowfall and below normal temperatures during the last one third of October,|allowed drought conditions to improve across several locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing D2-Severe drought conditions continued across the northwestern corner of McCone County, while the remainder of the northern third of the county was in D1-Moderate drought. At the end of the month, the far northwestern corner improved to D1-Moderate drought.
